meta.title: Designing a Frontend System from A to Z
meta.description: A visual, comprehensive, and easy-to-remember guide — covering requirement clarification, architecture, rendering, performance, real-time, and security. Includes diagrams, comparison tables, flashcards, and a question bank for self-assessment.

sections.title: What is Frontend System Design & the RADIO thinking framework?
sections.intro: Unlike backend system design (which focuses on scaling servers, databases, and throughput), frontend system design focuses on user experience: client architecture, data flow, rendering, perceived performance, and codebase maintainability.

blocks.html: In an interview, you typically have 45–60 minutes to design a client-side product (e.g., News Feed, Autocomplete, Chat, Photo Gallery). The interviewer evaluates how systematically you think, not how many APIs you remember.

blocks.text: The RADIO framework — the backbone of every answer.

blocks.html: RADIO is a popular mnemonic to help you not miss any steps. Proceed sequentially but be flexible to revisit steps when needed.

blocks.caption: The RADIO process

rows: Understand the problem: functionality, users, constraints, devices.
Draw the major components & data flow between them
State definition: server data vs. client/UI state
The contract between client and server, and between components (props).
Performance, a11y, network, UX, security — deep dive.

blocks.title: Interview Tips
blocks.body: Think out loud and continuously confirm with the interviewer: "I'll assume X, does that sound okay to you?" They evaluate the communication process and trade-offs more than a single "correct" answer.

blocks.title: Key points to remember
blocks.body: FE System Design = Intentional Trade-offs. There is no perfect solution; every choice comes with a cost. Your job is to pick the right one for the right context and explain why.

blocks.question: What is the core difference between frontend system design and backend system design?
options: Frontend doesn't need to worry about scale, only backend does.
FE focuses on user experience, client architecture, rendering, and perceived performance; backend focuses on throughput, database, and server scaling.
FE is not just about drawing UI; there is no "system design" involved.
The two types are completely identical.
blocks.explanation: Both require thinking about trade-offs and scale, but frontend design revolves around the client: how to organize components, manage state, optimize rendering, network, and perceived user experience.

sections.title: Requirements Clarification
sections.intro: This is the most important step, yet the most overlooked. Jumping straight into a solution without understanding the problem is the biggest trap in interviews.

items.title: Functional
items.body: What does the product do? Users can: create posts, like, comment, infinite scroll, upload images...

items.title: Non-functional
items.body: How well does the product perform? Load speed, smoothness, offline capability, a11y, SEO.

items.title: Constraints
items.body: Device (mobile/desktop), supported browsers, network (3G?), multilingual support, framework.

items: **Users & Scale:** Who uses it? How many concurrent users? Primary device types?
Core features: Which features are mandatory for MVP? Which are "nice-to-have"?
Data: Does the data change frequently? Is real-time required?
Performance: Are there specific targets (e.g., LCP < 2.5s)?
SEO: Does the page need to be indexed by Google? (Significantly impacts rendering pattern)
Offline: Does it need to work when there is no network connection?
Cross-platform: Web-only or need to share logic with mobile app?
i18n / a11y: Do you need to support multiple languages, RTL, and screen readers?

blocks.title: Common mistakes
blocks.body: Spending too little (or too much) time on this step. The goal is to finalize the scope within ~5 minutes to establish a clear "contract" for the remainder of the session.

blocks.title: Practical Tips
blocks.body: Write the finalized list of requirements in a corner of the board (or a separate cell). When diving deeper, you can always point to it to explain why a feature was chosen or omitted.

blocks.question: Which of the following requirements has the MOST DIRECT and SIGNIFICANT impact on choosing a rendering pattern (CSR/SSR/SSG)?
options: The theme colors of the application.
SEO requirements and first contentful paint (FCP/LCP) speed.
Number of buttons on the page
blocks.explanation: If strong SEO and fast initial content display are required, SSR/SSG usually wins over CSR. This is why you must ask about SEO from the requirements stage.

sections.title: Overall architecture & component separation
sections.intro: After understanding the problem, you outline the major "blocks" of the client system and how they communicate. Goal: separation of concerns — each part has a clear responsibility.

blocks.caption: The layered architecture of a Single Page Application (SPA)

blocks.html: Idea: data flows from Server → Network → Store → View Model → View, while events flow in the opposite direction. Each layer can be tested and replaced independently.

items.title: Container ("smart")
items.body: Knowledge of data & logic. Calling APIs, managing state, handling events. Not concerned with specific display.

items.title: Presentational ("dumb")
items.body: Only receives props and renders UI. No business state. Easy to test, easy to reuse, easy to build Storybook.

blocks.title: Why separate it like that?
blocks.body: Reusability (UI reused in multiple places), easy to test (logic separated from DOM), and easy to maintain (changing API does not affect UI and vice versa).

blocks.html: Micro-frontend splits a large application into multiple independent parts, each owned by a team (potentially using different frameworks). It is effective for **large organizations with many teams**, but increases complexity in build processes, duplicate bundles, and UX consistency.

items.text: - UX is most prone to inconsistency

blocks.question: The main benefit of separating Container and Presentational components is improved separation of concerns: Container components handle logic, state, and data fetching, while Presentational components focus solely on rendering UI. This makes code more reusable, testable, and easier to maintain.
options: Make the app run faster algorithmically.
Separate logic from presentation → easier to test, reuse, and maintain.
Reduce the number of lines of code by half.
blocks.explanation: This is about separation of concerns. Presentational components only render from props, making them extremely easy to test and reuse; containers hold the logic, so the data source can be changed without affecting the UI.

sections.title: Rendering Patterns: CSR · SSR · SSG · ISR
sections.intro: Core question: Where and when is HTML generated? This choice affects SEO, rendering speed, server cost, and complexity.

blocks.caption: When is HTML ready — CSR vs SSR

rows: In the browser, after JS runs
Dashboard, post-login app, minimal SEO needed.
For each request, on the server
Personalized page, data changes continuously, requires SEO.
E-commerce, many pages but updates are not continuous.

blocks.html: Hydration is the process of attaching JavaScript event handlers to server-generated HTML, turning a "static" page into an "interactive" one. The issue: it can be heavy and delay Time to Interactive (TTI). Techniques to reduce cost:

items: Streaming SSR: the server sends HTML in chunks, and the browser renders progressively instead of waiting for the entire page.
Progressive / Selective Hydration: hydrate critical parts first (e.g., the portion the user is currently viewing).
Islands Architecture: most of the page is static HTML, only a few interactive "islands" require JavaScript (Astro, Qwik).
RSC (React Server Components): components run on the server, no JavaScript is sent to the client for that part.

blocks.title: How to answer intelligently
blocks.body: Don't say "always use SSR." Instead, say: "Since the page needs SEO and content varies by user, I choose SSR; but for the post-login dashboard, CSR is sufficient because it doesn't need indexing." → This demonstrates context-driven thinking.

items.front: Hydration is the process of converting a static HTML page into an interactive web application by attaching event handlers and state to the server-rendered markup.
items.back: The process of attaching JS event handlers to server-rendered HTML, turning a static page into an interactive one.

items.front: TTFB stands for Time to First Byte, which is the time from when the client sends a request to when it receives the first byte from the server.
items.back: Time To First Byte — the time from when a request is made until the first byte is received from the server. SSR heavily depends on it.

items.front: Islands Architecture is a web architecture pattern that loads the page as static HTML, then selectively hydrates only the interactive components (the "islands") on the client side. This approach reduces the amount of JavaScript sent to the browser, improving initial load performance and time to interactive.
items.back: The page is mostly static HTML; only interactive "islands" load JS → drastically reduces JS sent to the client.

blocks.question: You are building a news blog that requires good SEO and extremely fast loading, with content updated several times a day. Which pattern is the most suitable?
options: ISR / SSG with revalidation — pre-built for fast CDN delivery, periodically regenerated when content changes.
SSR for every request — because the latest data is always needed.
No need to render, just JSON.
blocks.explanation: Content remains unchanged every second, so SSR per request is unnecessary (wastes server resources). SSG/ISR provides good CDN speed + SEO, with periodic revalidation for updates. SSR is acceptable, but ISR is more cost-effective.

sections.title: Performance & Core Web Vitals
sections.intro: Performance isn't just about being "fast"—it's about being fast at the moments users perceive. Google measures this through Core Web Vitals.

blocks.text: Core Web Vitals — 3 metrics to remember.

items.title: LCP
items.body: Largest Contentful Paint. The time it takes for the largest element to appear. Good: < 2.5s

items.title: INP
items.body: Interaction to Next Paint. Interaction response delay (replaces FID). Good: < 200ms

items.title: CLS (Cumulative Layout Shift)
items.body: Cumulative Layout Shift. Layout shift level. Good: < 0.1

blocks.caption: Performance optimization map

blocks.text:
Code splitting and lazy loading are techniques used to optimize the loading performance of web applications. Code splitting allows you to break your application into smaller chunks, which can be loaded on demand rather than all at once. This reduces the initial load time and improves the user experience.

Lazy loading is a strategy that delays the loading of non-essential resources until they are needed. This means that components or modules are only loaded when they are required, further enhancing performance and reducing the amount of data that needs to be transferred during the initial load. Together, these techniques help in creating more efficient and responsive applications.

blocks.html: Instead of sending all JavaScript at once, only load the necessary parts. This is one of the most effective optimizations for FCP/LCP.

blocks.label: React lazy

blocks.text: List Virtualization — a key technique for handling long lists.

blocks.html: Rendering 10,000 rows at once will kill performance. Virtualization only renders items within the viewport (plus a small buffer), reusing DOM elements when scrolling.

blocks.title: Don't optimize prematurely.
blocks.body: Always measure first, optimize later. Use Lighthouse, Performance tab, and React Profiler to identify real bottlenecks instead of guessing. Optimizing the wrong place wastes time and complicates the code.

items.back: Set width/height for images & videos, reserve space for ads/embeds, avoid inserting content above the content being viewed.
Debounce: only runs AFTER activation stops (search input). Throttle: runs at most N times per second (scroll, resize).

items.front: Tree-shaking is a term used in JavaScript module bundlers (like Webpack, Rollup, or ESBuild) to eliminate dead code—code that is imported but never used. It relies on the static structure of ES modules (import/export) to determine which exports are actually referenced. During the build process, unused exports are "shaken off" from the final bundle, reducing file size and improving load performance.
items.back: Bundlers remove unused code (dead code) based on ES module imports, resulting in smaller bundles.

blocks.question: A page displaying a list of 50,000 products lags heavily when scrolling. What is the most optimal solution?
options: List virtualization (windowing) — only render items within the viewport
Use inline styles instead of classes.
blocks.explanation: The problem is too many DOM nodes. Virtualization keeps the DOM at around 10–20 items regardless of list size, enabling smooth scrolling and saving memory.

sections.title: Networking & Client-side API Design
sections.intro: Client-server communication significantly impacts UX. You need to know when to use REST, GraphQL, and how to handle pagination, retry, and race conditions.

blocks.text:
REST vs GraphQL

REST (Representational State Transfer) and GraphQL are two different approaches to building APIs.

REST is an architectural style that uses standard HTTP methods (GET, POST, PUT, DELETE) to interact with resources. Each resource is identified by a unique URL, and the server responds with the entire resource representation. This can lead to over-fetching or under-fetching of data, as clients may receive more or less information than needed.

GraphQL, on the other hand, is a query language for APIs that allows clients to request exactly the data they need. It provides a single endpoint and enables clients to specify the structure of the response. This reduces over-fetching and under-fetching issues, as clients can tailor their requests to their specific requirements.

In summary, REST is resource-oriented and can lead to inefficiencies in data retrieval, while GraphQL is query-oriented and offers more flexibility in data fetching.

rows: 1 endpoint, client declares the required fields themselves.
Complex UI with multiple data relationships.

items.title: Offset / Page
items.body: ?page=2&limit=20. Easy to understand, supports page navigation. Drawback: data inconsistency when new items are inserted.

items.title: Cursor-based
items.body: Stable with changing data, suitable for infinite scroll and feeds. Difficult to jump to arbitrary pages.

items.title: Infinite Scroll
items.body: UI loads more when scrolling to the end (IntersectionObserver), typically using a cursor below.

blocks.title: This is a commonly asked question.
blocks.body: "Which pagination should social media feeds use?" → Cursor-based. Because feeds continuously have new posts inserted at the top; offset would cause duplicate/missed items when scrolling.

blocks.html: When a user types "rea" quickly → "react", the request for "rea" may arrive AFTER "react" and overwrite the correct result. Solution:

blocks.label: AbortController

blocks.html: Combine with debounce (~300ms) to reduce the number of requests, and check "whether this request is still the latest" before calling setState.

items: Timeout: do not leave requests hanging indefinitely — cancel after N seconds.
Retry with exponential backoff: retry at 1s → 2s → 4s to avoid overwhelming the server.
Idempotency: For POST requests that create data, use an idempotency key to retry without creating duplicates.
Graceful degradation: serve stale cache / display user-friendly error messages instead of a white screen.

blocks.question: In an autocomplete field, how do you ensure the displayed results always match the latest keyword the user has typed?
options: Increase the timeout for every request.
Debounce input + cancel old requests using AbortController (or ignore non-latest responses)
Send all requests in parallel and display whichever returns first.
Type only one character per second.
blocks.explanation: This is a classic race condition. Debounce reduces the number of requests; AbortController cancels outdated requests so that old responses do not overwrite new results.

blocks.title: Common Misunderstandings
blocks.body: Many people stuff server data into Redux and write their own cache/loading/error logic. In reality, server state is very different from client state: it's asynchronous, can become "stale", and requires refetching & deduplication. Let React Query/SWR handle that.

blocks.html: One-way data flow makes debugging easier: every state change goes through action → reducer, so we always know "why the state changed."

blocks.question: A list of blog posts is fetched from the API and needs caching, auto-refetch when stale, and request deduplication. Which state type and tool should you use?
options: Server state — use React Query / SWR to handle caching, refetching, and deduplication automatically.
blocks.explanation: Data from APIs is server state. Libraries like React Query/SWR are specifically designed to handle caching, stale-while-revalidate, deduplication, and retry — eliminating the need to implement these yourself.

sections.title: Component Design & API Design
sections.intro: A good component is like a good function: easy to use correctly, hard to misuse. In interviews, designing a "props API" for a component (such as Autocomplete, Modal) is a very common question.

items: Single Responsibility: each component does one thing well.
Composition over configuration: prioritize composing smaller components rather than a single massive component with 30 props.
Controlled vs Uncontrolled: allow both when appropriate (value + onChange, or defaultValue).
Sensible defaults: works immediately with minimal props.
Default accessibility: ARIA roles and focus management are built-in.

blocks.text: Example: design an API for the Autocomplete component.

blocks.label: Props API

blocks.title: Advantages during the interview
blocks.body: When designing Autocomplete, proactively mention: debounce, race condition (AbortController), caching results, keyboard navigation (↑↓ Enter Esc), ARIA (role="combobox", aria-activedescendant), and virtualization for long lists. This is the checklist interviewers expect.

blocks.text: The Compound Components pattern is a design pattern in React that allows you to create components that work together as a single unit. This pattern enables better composition and flexibility by allowing child components to communicate with their parent component without needing to pass props explicitly. It promotes a more intuitive API and enhances the reusability of components.

blocks.html: Allows users to compose child components flexibly, sharing state implicitly via Context.

blocks.title: Design thinking
blocks.body:
**Question:** "What customizations will users of this component need?"  
Too few props → rigid. Too many props → hard to use. Compound components and render props/children help balance flexibility without exploding props.

blocks.question: When designing an Autocomplete component for an interview, which of the following factors is OFTEN FORGOTTEN but highly valued?
options: Select the background color for the dropdown.
Keyboard navigation + ARIA (role combobox) + race condition handling + debounce
blocks.explanation: The "difficult" and impressive part lies in accessibility (keyboard navigation, ARIA), asynchronous handling (race conditions, debounce, cache).

sections.intro: Proper caching helps apps run faster and remain functional even on weak networks. However, "there are only two hard problems in computer science: naming things and cache invalidation."

blocks.title: Token security
blocks.body: Don't store sensitive tokens in localStorage if you're concerned about XSS — it's accessible via JavaScript. Cookies with HttpOnly + Secure + SameSite flags are safer for sessions, since JavaScript cannot read them.

blocks.html: Stale-while-revalidate (core of SWR/React Query): immediately returns stale data to the UI for smoothness, while fetching in the background to update → users see fast results and always have fresh data.

blocks.question: You need to store several hundred MB of data for the app to work offline. Which mechanism is suitable?
options: IndexedDB (combined with Service Worker + Cache API for assets)
blocks.explanation: localStorage is limited to ~5–10MB and is synchronous (blocks the main thread). IndexedDB can store large amounts of structured data asynchronously; combined with Service Workers, it enables full offline support.

sections.intro: Chat, notifications, stock prices, "typing..." — when to use polling, SSE, or WebSocket? Each has its own trade-offs in latency, data direction, and complexity.

blocks.title: How to quickly select
blocks.body: Server push only? → SSE (lightweight, auto-reconnect, over HTTP). Need high-frequency bidirectional? → WebSocket. Not urgent/simple? → Polling. Always consider reconnection, heartbeat, and fallback.

blocks.text: Optimistic UI for a real-time feel.

blocks.html: When liking a post, update the UI immediately before the server responds, then rollback if an error occurs. The user experiences instant feedback even with network latency.

blocks.question: You are building a 1-on-1 chat feature with "typing..." indicators and instant two-way messaging. What is the most suitable mechanism?
blocks.explanation: The chat requires instant two-way sending/receiving (messages + typing indicator). SSE is one-way only (server → client). WebSocket provides full-duplex, low latency — exactly what's needed.

sections.title: Accessibility (a11y) and Internationalization (i18n)
sections.intro: A good system serves all users — including those using screen readers, keyboards, and people from different languages/cultures. This is what distinguishes an average candidate from an excellent one.

items.title: Keyboard
items.body: All operations can be performed using the keyboard. Focus is clearly visible, tab order is logical, and there is no focus trap.

items.title: Semantic HTML
items.body: Use correct semantic tags (button, nav, main) before considering ARIA. Proper HTML equals free accessibility.

items.title: ARIA & screen reader
items.body: aria-label, role, aria-live for dynamic content. Only use ARIA when HTML is insufficient.

items.title: Contrast & Motion
items.body: Color contrast meets WCAG (4.5:1), respects prefers-reduced-motion.

blocks.title: The first rule of ARIA
blocks.body: "No ARIA is better than bad ARIA." Prioritize semantic HTML. A real `<button>` already has built-in focus, keyboard, and role — no need for `<div onClick>` then patching with ARIA.

items: String externalization: do not hardcode text; use translation keys.
Plural & gender: "1 item" vs "2 items" — use ICU MessageFormat.
Format according to locale: dates, numbers, and currency via the Intl API.
RTL: right-to-left support (Arabic, Hebrew) — use logical CSS properties (margin-inline-start).
Lazy-load language packs: only load the current locale to reduce bundle size.

blocks.question: What is the best way to create an accessible button?
options: Use semantic `<button>` tags — they come with built-in keyboard support, focus, and role.
<div onClick> and then add a lot of ARIA attributes.
The image has a click event.
blocks.explanation: Semantic HTML is always the first choice. The `<button>` element already supports focus, Enter/Space keys, and is announced with the correct role by screen readers. "No ARIA is better than bad ARIA."

sections.title: Frontend Security
sections.intro: Frontend cannot fully protect itself (everything on the client side can be spoofed), but there are vulnerabilities that FE engineers must guard against: XSS, CSRF, and sensitive information exposure.

rows: Inject malicious script into the page via unescaped input.
Escape output, avoid dangerouslySetInnerHTML, use CSP, sanitize (DOMPurify).
Trick the browser into sending a request with cookies to another site.
SameSite cookie, CSRF token, Origin check
Embed a transparent iframe site to trick clicks.
API key/token embedded in client bundle
Never expose server-side secrets in client code; use a proxy instead.

blocks.text: XSS — the number one enemy of Frontend.

blocks.title: React helps you (partially)
blocks.body: React automatically escapes values in JSX, so `{userInput}` is safe against XSS. Danger only arises when you use `dangerouslySetInnerHTML` — in that case, you MUST sanitize with DOMPurify first.

blocks.title: The golden rule
blocks.body: Never trust data from the client. All frontend validation is only for good UX; real validation must happen on the server. The frontend can be opened via DevTools and anything can be modified.

blocks.question: When must you **mandatorily** sanitize HTML (e.g., using DOMPurify) in React?
options: Every time {variable} is rendered in JSX
When using dangerouslySetInnerHTML with user-provided content
blocks.explanation: JSX `{variable}` is automatically escaped by React, making it safe. XSS risks arise when you inject raw HTML via `dangerouslySetInnerHTML` — you must sanitize it first.

blocks.text: Reflow vs Repaint — why is it important?

rows: Resizing, repositioning, adding/removing DOM elements, reading offsetHeight...
Expensive — must recalculate the geometry of the entire tree.
Change color, visibility, box-shadow (no layout change)
Cheapest — GPU compositing layers, skipping Layout & Paint

blocks.title: Layout Thrashing
blocks.body: Reading layout properties (offsetTop, getBoundingClientRect) immediately after writing to the DOM forces the browser to perform synchronous reflows repeatedly. To avoid this: batch all reads together before writing (read-then-write batching), or use requestAnimationFrame.

blocks.title: A high-scoring answer.
blocks.body: "For smooth 60fps animation, I only animate transform and opacity because they run on the compositor thread, do not block the main thread, and do not cause reflow." → demonstrates your understanding of the pipeline.

blocks.question: Which CSS property, when animated, will NOT cause reflow or repaint, only composite?
blocks.explanation: `transform` and `opacity` are processed on the compositor thread on the GPU, completely bypassing the Layout and Paint steps. All other properties change geometry, triggering reflow.

sections.title: Event Loop & Asynchronous in JS
sections.intro: JavaScript runs on a single thread but can handle thousands of tasks thanks to the event loop. Understanding the order of microtasks vs macrotasks helps you debug those "hard-to-understand" bugs related to execution order.

blocks.caption: How does the event loop coordinate?

blocks.label: JS

blocks.title: **The Unforgettable Rule**
blocks.body: After each macrotask, the event loop clears the ENTIRE microtask queue before rendering and fetching the next macrotask. Therefore, Promise.then always runs before setTimeout, even if setTimeout is set to 0ms.

blocks.title: Trap: infinite microtask
blocks.body: If a microtask continuously creates new microtasks, rendering will suffer from starvation — the page freezes because the event loop never reaches the paint step. For repeated heavy tasks, use setTimeout/requestIdleCallback to yield the thread.

blocks.question: The output of: setTimeout(()=>log('A'),0); Promise.resolve().then(()=>log('B')); log('C') is: C, B, A
blocks.explanation: log('C') runs synchronously first. Next, microtask B (Promise) is cleared before anything else. Finally, macrotask A (setTimeout) runs. → C, B, A.

sections.title: Network protocol: HTTP/1.1 → HTTP/3
sections.intro: Each HTTP version addresses a specific bottleneck. Understanding them helps you explain why bundling was once important, and why with HTTP/2, "file concatenation" is no longer necessary.

rows: 1 request/response per connection at a time; must open multiple parallel connections.
Head-of-line blocking; limit of ~6 connections per domain → requires bundling, sprites.
Multiplexing: multiple streams over a single connection; header compression; server push.
Still HOL blocking at the TCP layer (losing one packet blocks the entire connection).
Runs on QUIC (UDP); each stream is independent; faster handshake.
New, some older networks block UDP.

blocks.title: Practical Optimization
blocks.body: Use `<link rel="preconnect">` for critical third-party domains (CDN, API), and `dns-prefetch` for less important domains. This completes DNS/TCP/TLS early, shaving off a few hundred milliseconds when the actual request occurs.

blocks.title: Common interview questions
blocks.body: "With HTTP/2, should you still bundle all JS into one file?" → No. Multiplexing eliminates the penalty for multiple small files, and actually benefits caching (fixing one module won't invalidate the cache for the entire bundle).

blocks.question: HTTP/2 reduces the need to bundle multiple JS files into one large bundle because it supports multiplexing, allowing multiple requests to be sent concurrently over a single TCP connection. This eliminates the overhead of multiple connections and reduces latency, making it efficient to load many small files individually rather than combining them into a single large file.
options: HTTP/2 compresses files better, so large files are not an issue.
Multiplexing allows parallel loading of multiple files over a single connection, without the penalties of HTTP/1.1.
HTTP/2 does not support large files.
blocks.explanation: HTTP/1.1 limits the number of connections and processes requests sequentially, so bundling files helps reduce the number of requests. HTTP/2 multiplexing loads multiple streams in parallel over a single connection, so splitting files into smaller ones is better for caching.

blocks.html: V8 does not know your object's "shape" in advance. It creates a hidden class (shape) for each object structure. Objects with the same shape share a hidden class → property access is extremely fast. Changing the shape (adding/deleting properties, adding in a different order) breaks this optimization.

blocks.label: JS

blocks.title: Common memory leaks in frontend development
blocks.body: Listener not removed, setInterval not cleared, closure holding large references, or detached DOM nodes still held by JavaScript. In React: forgetting cleanup in useEffect. Use DevTools → Memory to take heap snapshots and find leaks.

blocks.title: Don't blindly micro-optimize.
blocks.body: The engine is extremely intelligent; writing clear code with consistent shape and avoiding deletions in hot paths is sufficient. Measure with a profiler before optimizing — "premature optimization is the root of all evil."

blocks.question: Why should all properties of an object be initialized in the same order, within the constructor?
options: To share the same hidden class (shape) among objects, helping V8 optimize property access.
blocks.explanation: Objects with the same shape share a hidden class → inline caching works, property access is fast. Adding dynamic properties or changing the order creates a new shape, breaking optimization.

sections.title: Detailed HTTP Caching & CORS
sections.intro: Proper caching is a "free" way to boost performance and reduce server load. CORS is something every frontend developer has struggled with — understanding it helps you debug quickly instead of "trial and error."

rows: Cache for 1 year, no revalidation needed — for assets with content hash.
Must revalidate with the server before using (NOT "no cache")
Absolutely do not store — for sensitive data.
Content fingerprinting; server returns 304 Not Modified if unchanged → saves bandwidth.
Use the old version immediately, update silently — fast + gradually new

blocks.title: Classic caching strategy
blocks.body: Files with content hashes (e.g., app.a3f9.js) are cached immutably for 1 year because changing content changes the filename. The index.html file uses no-cache (always revalidate) so users receive the latest version. This is the foundation of safe deployment.

blocks.title: CORS is a browser mechanism.
blocks.body: CORS does not protect the server (requests still reach the server). It protects the user: the browser blocks JavaScript from reading responses from a different origin unless the server allows it. Therefore, CORS errors must be fixed on the server (by adding headers), not on the client.

blocks.title: Trick question
blocks.body: "Does no-cache mean no caching?" → No! no-cache = still stores but must revalidate (ETag) before using. "Don't store anything" is no-store. Confusing these two is a very common mistake.

blocks.question: A CORS error appears in the console. Where is the correct place to fix it?
options: Fix client-side JavaScript code to "bypass" CORS
Configure the server to return the correct Access-Control-Allow-Origin header (or use a proxy).
blocks.explanation: CORS is enforced by the browser based on headers from the server. The client cannot "disable" it. The server must allow the origin, or the request must go through a same-origin proxy.

sections.title: Design System & Component Library
sections.intro: As the product and team grow, UI consistency becomes a technical challenge. A design system serves as the "single source of truth," enabling dozens of developers to create consistent interfaces without stepping on each other's toes.

      <text x="350" y="221" text-anchor="middle" fill="#be185d" font-size="12" font-weight="600">The real product</text>
    </svg>
blocks.caption: From token to product

blocks.text: Design Tokens — the heart of theming.

blocks.label: CSS

blocks.title: Versioning & Breaking Changes
blocks.body: Component libraries should use semantic versioning. Changing a required prop constitutes a major version. Provide codemods for automatic migration, and issue deprecation warnings at least one version before removal. This maintains trust with the teams using the library.

blocks.title: Notable trade-off
blocks.body: "Flexible" components (many props) vs. "opinionated" components (fewer choices, high consistency). Good libraries often use compound components and slots to achieve both flexibility and consistency, rather than cramming in 30 boolean props.

blocks.question: Why should "semantic tokens" (--color-primary) be separated from "primitive tokens" (--blue-500)?
options: To make the CSS file smaller
To change the theme/brand by simply pointing to a different semantic token, without modifying individual components.
blocks.explanation: The component only references semantic tokens. When switching themes (light/dark, brand change), you simply point the semantic tokens to different primitive tokens, and all components update accordingly.

sections.title: Testing Strategy for Frontend
sections.intro: Tests are not about achieving 100% coverage, but about having confidence to refactor and deploy. The core question: what to test, at which layer, to catch the most bugs with the lowest maintenance cost.

blocks.svg:

blocks.caption: Distribute tests by layer

blocks.text: What type of tests catch which bugs?

rows: Pure function, util, reducer, custom hook
Component renders correctly, user interaction, callback invocation

blocks.title: The philosophy of Testing Library
blocks.body: "Test like how users interact" — find elements by role/label/text instead of class or internal test-id. Such tests are less brittle during refactoring and catch real bugs. Avoid testing implementation details (internal state, function names).

blocks.title: Trap: flaky tests
blocks.body: E2E tests become "flaky" due to timing issues (waiting for animations, network). Mitigation: wait for conditions (await expect(...).toBeVisible()) instead of sleep(2000), mock network when needed, and isolate tests (no dependency on execution order).

blocks.question: According to the philosophy of React Testing Library, which element-finding method is most recommended?
options: Through the role/label/text that the user can see
Based on the DOM order (the 3rd element)
blocks.explanation: RTL encourages querying based on what users perceive (role, label, text), making tests resilient to refactoring and reflecting real user experience. Internal class/state details are implementation specifics.

sections.title: Monitoring and Observability
sections.intro: "You cannot improve what you don't measure." After deployment, how do you know if real users encounter errors or if the page is slow? This is what distinguishes junior from senior engineers.

items.title: Error Tracking
items.body: Catch JS runtime errors, promise rejections, and API errors. Group them by category, include stack trace and breadcrumbs. Tools: Sentry, Rollbar.

items.title: RUM — Real User Monitoring
items.body: Measure Core Web Vitals (LCP, INP, CLS) from real users, segmented by device/network/region. This differs from lab tests (Lighthouse).

items.title: Analytics & Funnel
items.body: At which step did the user drop off, and which feature was used? Connect performance to user behavior and revenue.

blocks.text: Performance Budget — set limits to avoid "drifting."

blocks.label: JSON

blocks.title: Lab data vs Field data
blocks.body: Lab (Lighthouse) = simulated environment, stable, good for debugging. Field/RUM = real users, many device/network variations — these are the numbers that matter for SEO (Google uses CrUX). Must look at both.

blocks.title: Source maps for production
blocks.body: Upload private source maps (not public) to Sentry so that stack traces from minified code become readable. Do not deploy source maps publicly to avoid source code exposure.

blocks.question: Google uses field data from the Chrome User Experience Report (CrUX) to evaluate Core Web Vitals for search rankings.
options: Lighthouse scores run on your machine.
Number of lines of code on the page
blocks.explanation: Google uses the Chrome User Experience Report (CrUX) — field data from real Chrome users. Lighthouse provides lab data, useful for debugging but not directly used for ranking.

sections.title: Build, Bundling & Deploy
sections.intro: The code you write doesn't run directly in the browser. Understanding bundlers helps you reduce bundle size, improve load speed, and deploy safely with feature flags and rollback.

rows: Remove dead code — requires ES modules for static analysis.
Split bundles by route/component, load on demand (dynamic import()).
Minify variable names, remove whitespace, dead code
File name with hash (app.a3f9.js) → permanent cache, content change = name change
Share the runtime module between apps (micro-frontend platform).

blocks.caption: From commit to production safely

blocks.title: Feature flags = decoupling deployment from release
blocks.body: Deploy code to production but disable the feature behind a flag. Gradually enable it from 1% → 50% → 100%. If an error occurs, turn off the flag immediately (no need to rollback the deployment). Also used for A/B testing.

blocks.title: A valuable question.
blocks.body: How to deploy without breaking the experience for users on an old tab? → Use versioned assets with content hashes, keep old files for a period of time, and handle "chunk load failed" errors by reloading the page when a new version is detected.

blocks.question: The main benefit of feature flags compared to using deploy/rollback is that they allow you to toggle features on or off at runtime without redeploying, enabling safer, more granular control over releases, faster rollback, and easier A/B testing or gradual rollouts.
options: Separate code deployment from feature activation — enable/disable instantly without redeploying.
blocks.explanation: Feature flags allow code to be in production while the feature remains disabled; they enable gradual activation and immediate deactivation upon errors without requiring deployment or rollback. Ideal for gradual rollouts and A/B testing.

sections.title: Bundler comparison: Webpack · Vite · esbuild · Rollup
sections.intro: Each build tool is created to solve a different problem. Understanding them helps you choose the right tool and explains why Vite is "lightning fast" during development.

rows: Bundle everything; extensive configuration; module federation
Dev: Native ESM + esbuild, no bundling; Prod: Rollup
10–100× faster than JS tools; fewer advanced features

blocks.text: Why is Vite fast during development?

blocks.caption: Webpack bundles everything upfront, while Vite serves ESM on demand.

blocks.title: The core idea of Vite
blocks.body: Modern browsers support native ES modules. During development, Vite does not bundle but lets the browser import each module individually; Vite only transforms the modules that are requested (using esbuild for extremely fast processing). As a result, server startup and HMR are nearly instantaneous, regardless of project size. For production builds, Vite bundles using Rollup for optimization.

blocks.title: How to choose a tool
blocks.body: For building libraries → Rollup (clean output, good tree-shaking). For building new apps → Vite (excellent DX). For legacy/complex projects requiring specific plugins → Webpack. For raw speed in the pipeline → esbuild.

blocks.question: Why does Vite start the dev server almost instantly even with large projects?
options: Because it bundles 1000 times faster than Webpack.
When the dev does NOT bundle — uses the browser's native ESM, only transforms the requested module.
Because it does not require JavaScript.
Because it runs on a dedicated server.
blocks.explanation: Webpack bundles everything before running, so large projects start slowly. Vite leverages native ESM, only building modules requested by the browser (via esbuild) → instant startup & HMR.

sections.title: Advanced Testing: Mock, Async & Contract
sections.intro: After mastering the testing pyramid, here are the techniques that distinguish developers who write "runnable" tests from those who write "reliable" tests. Testing incorrectly is worse than not testing at all, as it creates a false sense of security.

blocks.text: Mock vs Stub vs Spy — don't get confused.

rows: Replace the function with a fixed return value.
Need a dependency that returns predefined data (mock API returns an object).
Track how the ACTUAL function is called (number of times, parameters).
To confirm whether the callback/analytics is called correctly.
A mock object has expectations about how it is called.
Check interaction between modules, fail if called incorrectly.
Replace the heavy system with a lightweight version.

blocks.title: Don't over-mock.
blocks.body: Mocking everything makes tests only verify "the code calls the mocked function correctly" — it doesn't reflect real behavior. Prioritize mocking at system boundaries (network, time, storage), and keep the logic within the test running as real code.

blocks.label: JS

blocks.title: Mock the network with MSW
blocks.body: Mock Service Worker (MSW) intercepts requests at the network layer, so components use real fetch without needing code changes. The same mock works for both testing and development — preventing tests from "knowing too much" about how the API is called.

blocks.text: Contract Testing — preventing "API changes without anyone knowing"

blocks.html: When frontend and backend develop independently, contract tests ensure both sides still "understand each other": the frontend declares what response format it expects, and the backend confirms it provides that exact format. If the backend changes a field, the contract test fails immediately — catching the error before production.

blocks.title: A high-scoring answer.
blocks.body: I test based on user behavior, mock at the network boundary using MSW, use fake timers for debounce/throttle, and apply contract tests for the FE-BE boundary. → This shows you understand testing at the correct layers.

blocks.question: The main difference between "spy" and "stub" is that a spy records information about how a function is called (e.g., number of calls, arguments), while a stub replaces a function with a predefined behavior or return value, typically to isolate the code under test.
options: There is no difference, just the name.
Spy monitors how the real function is called; stub replaces the function with a predefined return value.
Spy is only used for async, stub for sync.
blocks.explanation: Spy observes the real function (how many times it is called, what parameters are passed) without altering its behavior; stub replaces the function entirely with a fixed value/implementation.

sections.title: Storybook & Component Documentation
sections.intro: Isolating components from the app enables efficient development, testing, and documentation. Storybook is the standard tool for building a living library for a design system.

items.title: Isolated development
items.body: Build each component state (loading, error, empty, full) without running the entire app or creating complex data.

items.title: Living document
items.body: Each "story" is a live example. Designers and developers can view all variants, props, and usage patterns.

items.title: Visual regression
items.body: Take screenshots of each story, compare when changes occur → catch UI "breakage" that unit tests cannot detect.

items.title: A11y audit
items.body: Addon to check accessibility directly in the story: contrast, ARIA, focus order.

blocks.label: JS

blocks.title: Story = test + docs + dev simultaneously
blocks.body: A good story serves as documentation (for others to view), a playground (for developers to experiment), and input for visual regression tests. "Write once, use for three purposes" — this is why Storybook is worth investing in for teams with a design system.

blocks.title: Systems thinking
blocks.body: Mentioning Storybook when asked about design systems shows you're thinking about maintainability and collaboration, not just making code work. That's the mindset of someone building platform/infra for a team.

blocks.question: What is the "3-in-1" benefit of a story in Storybook?
options: It serves as a living document, a development playground, and an input for visual regression.
blocks.explanation: A story is a live example → it serves as documentation, allows developers to test in isolation, and acts as a snapshot for visual regression testing.

sections.title: Module Federation — executing micro-frontends
sections.intro: This is the most common technical mechanism for implementing micro-frontends: allowing multiple apps to build and deploy independently while sharing code and rendering each other at runtime.

blocks.caption: The host loads the module from a remote source at runtime.

rows: Declare shared resources (React, common libraries) to load once, avoiding multiple versions being downloaded.
Set singleton: true for React → prevents two React instances from conflicting.
Error boundary + fallback UI; don't let one remote crash the entire app.
Share the design system via a shared package.

blocks.title: The cost to pay
blocks.body: Module Federation adds significant complexity: managing shared dependencies, runtime versioning, difficulty debugging when remotes fail, and often worse performance than a monolith due to loading multiple runtimes. Only use it when the organizational benefits (independent teams) outweigh this cost.

blocks.title: Alternative
blocks.body: Not every micro-frontend requires Module Federation runtime. For lighter requirements: build-time integration (each team exports a package), iframe (strong isolation but limited UX), or web components. Choose based on the actual level of independence needed.

blocks.question: Why is it necessary to declare React as a shared singleton in Module Federation?
options: To avoid loading multiple React versions simultaneously — multiple React versions break hooks and context.
To reduce the number of configuration lines
blocks.explanation: If the host and remote each load a separate React instance, hooks and context will break (React requires a single instance). Setting `singleton: true` ensures only one React instance is shared.

sections.title: Distributed Tracing & Alert Strategy
sections.intro: When a request flows through FE → API gateway → multiple services, how do you identify which step is slow? Tracing connects the pieces into a single picture. And alert properly to avoid being "ignored" due to too many alerts.

blocks.svg:
<svg viewBox="0 0 700 200" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" font-family="Space Grotesk, sans-serif">
      <text x="30" y="30" fill="#8a8a82" font-size="10" font-family="JetBrains Mono, monospace">trace-id: abc123</text>
      <rect x="30" y="42" width="620" height="26" rx="5" fill="#eceae3" stroke="#0d7d72"></rect>
      <text x="40" y="59" fill="#0d7d72" font-size="10" font-weight="600">FE — page load (1200ms)</text>

      <rect x="90" y="78" width="380" height="24" rx="5" fill="#eceae3" stroke="#b45309"></rect>
      <text x="100" y="94" fill="#b45309" font-size="9.5" font-weight="600">span: API gateway /feed (820ms)</text>

      <rect x="140" y="112" width="180" height="24" rx="5" fill="#eceae3" stroke="#4f46e5"></rect>
      <text x="150" y="128" fill="#4f46e5" font-size="9" font-weight="600">span: auth-service (110ms)</text>

      <rect x="330" y="112" width="120" height="24" rx="5" fill="#eceae3" stroke="#be185d"></rect>
      <text x="338" y="128" fill="#be185d" font-size="9" font-weight="600">span: db query (560ms)</text>

      <text x="350" y="170" text-anchor="middle" fill="#8a8a82" font-size="10.5">Looking at the waterfall, I can tell immediately. <tspan fill="#be185d" font-weight="600">db query 560ms</tspan> It’s a bottleneck — don’t guess blindly.</text>
    </svg>
blocks.caption: A trace consists of multiple spans that are sequential/nested.

blocks.title: The role of Frontend
blocks.body: The frontend initializes a trace-id and sends it as a header (e.g., traceparent per W3C Trace Context standard) with every API call. This links user-facing errors/latency to backend logs — making it feasible to debug "why user X experienced slowness."

items.title: Logs
items.body: Discrete events have timestamps. Good for investigating a specific case in detail.

items.title: Metrics
items.body: Time-series aggregated metrics (p50/p95/p99 latency, error rate). Suitable for dashboards and alerts.

items.title: Traces
items.body: The journey of a request through services. Useful for identifying bottlenecks in a distributed system.

rows: Alert on symptoms, not on root causes.
Report "error rate > 2%" (affects users) instead of "high CPU" (may be harmless).
p95/p99 reflects the worst-case experience; averages hide the long tail.
Each alert must have a clear action; if nothing can be done, do not alert.
Too sensitive → false alarms (cry wolf); too loose → missed real incidents.

blocks.title: Why p95 is more important than the average.
blocks.body: "Average 200ms" sounds fine, but if p99 is 5 seconds, then 1% of users are suffering — often the most important ones (heavy data users). Seniors always look at tail latency, not just the average.

blocks.question: Why should alerts be based on p95/p99 latency instead of average latency?
options: Because the average is more difficult to calculate.
Averages hide the tail — a small group of users can have a very poor experience while the average still looks good.
Since p99 is always smaller than the average
Since the average does not measure latency
blocks.explanation: A few extremely slow requests get "diluted" in the average, but p95/p99 exposes them. Tail latency often affects users with the most data.

sections.title: Optimize Images & Resources
sections.intro: Images often account for 50%+ of page weight. Proper optimization is the fastest way to improve LCP. This is a "low-hanging fruit" that many overlook.

blocks.text: Image formats — choose the right one.

rows: Infinite resolution, small, can be styled with CSS.

blocks.text:
Responsive images and lazy loading are techniques used to optimize web performance and improve user experience. 

Responsive images allow different image sizes to be served based on the user's device and screen resolution, ensuring that images are displayed at the appropriate size without unnecessary loading of large files on smaller screens.

Lazy loading defers the loading of images until they are needed, typically when they come into the viewport. This reduces initial page load time and saves bandwidth, as images that are not immediately visible are not loaded until the user scrolls down to them. 

Implementing these techniques can significantly enhance the performance of a website, especially on mobile devices.

blocks.label: HTML

blocks.title: Always set width & height.
blocks.body: Declare width/height (or aspect-ratio) so the browser reserves space in advance — preventing layout shifts when images finish loading (improving CLS). Images without dimensions are the most common cause of CLS.

blocks.title: LCP images should NOT be lazy-loaded.
blocks.body: Images "above the fold" (typically LCP images) should use `loading="eager"` + `fetchpriority="high"`, and even preload. Lazy-loading them will worsen LCP. Only lazy-load images below the fold.

blocks.question: Which images should have `loading="lazy"` applied?
options: Large hero image at the top of the page (LCP image)
The image is below the first screen; users have to scroll to see it.
blocks.explanation: Lazy-load defers image loading until near the viewport — good for off-screen images. Lazy-loading LCP/above-the-fold images will slow down LCP.

sections.title: PWA, Service Worker & Offline-first
sections.intro: A Service Worker is a background proxy between the app and the network, enabling the app to work offline, load instantly on subsequent visits, and receive push notifications. It is the foundation of an "app-like" experience.

blocks.text: Where does the Service Worker stand?

blocks.svg:

blocks.caption: SW is a proxy between the app and the network.

rows: Fetch from cache first, only go to network if not available.
Go to network first, use cache on error.
Data needs to be fresh (feed, price)
Return cache immediately, while updating in the background.
Avatar, content changes infrequently — fast + gradual updates.

blocks.title: Trap: SW "stuck" on an old version
blocks.body: Service Workers have their own lifecycle (install → waiting → activate). If updates are not handled properly, users may get stuck on an old version. A strategy of skip-waiting combined with a "New version available, reload?" notification is needed for smooth updates.

blocks.title: Requirements of PWA
blocks.body: To be installable as an app, you need HTTPS, a Web App Manifest (name, icon, theme), and a Service Worker. When these are in place, the browser shows an "Add to Home Screen" prompt.

blocks.question: The most suitable caching strategy for feed data that must always be fresh but still needs a fallback when offline is **stale-while-revalidate**. This approach serves cached data immediately (stale) while fetching fresh data in the background, ensuring the user sees content even without a network connection. For critical freshness, combine it with a short max-age and a network-first strategy, falling back to cache only when the network fails.
blocks.explanation: Network First prioritizes fetching fresh data from the network, only falling back to cache when offline or on error — suitable for feeds. Cache First returns stale content.

sections.title: Frontend in a large team scale.
sections.intro: When a codebase has hundreds of developers, the issue is no longer individual technical skill but how to organize so that many people can work in parallel without conflicts or slowing each other down.

rows: A PR that fixes both the app and the library.
Need smart tools (Nx, Turborepo) to only build the changed parts.

blocks.title: Don't use it just because it "sounds cool."
blocks.body: Micro-frontends solve organizational problems (multiple teams deploying independently), not technical problems. The cost: complexity in shared dependencies, UI consistency, and performance (loading multiple runtimes). Only worthwhile when teams are large enough to block each other.

blocks.title: Senior mindset
blocks.body: "I'll start with a modular monolith with clear module boundaries. Only split into micro-frontends when there's evidence that teams are blocking each other during deployment." → shows you prioritize simpler solutions first.

blocks.question: The most reasonable reason to adopt a micro-frontend architecture is to enable independent development, deployment, and scaling of frontend features by different teams, reducing coordination overhead and improving maintainability for large-scale applications.
options: Allows multiple teams to develop and deploy independently without blocking each other.
blocks.explanation: Micro-frontend is a solution for team organization/scaling issues, traded off by increased technical complexity and often worse performance. The main benefit is team independence.

sections.title: Case Study: Chat Application (like Messenger)
sections.intro: A classic interview question. A chat app combines nearly every topic: real-time, optimistic UI, virtualized list, offline, and state synchronization. We follow the RADIO framework.

blocks.caption: Client architecture of chat

blocks.label: JS

items.title: Optimistic send
items.body: Display the message immediately with a "sending" status and a temporary ID. When the server acknowledges, replace it with the real ID. On error, mark it as failed and allow retry.

items.title: Virtual list
items.body: Only render messages within the viewport. Load more on reverse scroll (infinite scroll upward), maintain scroll position.

items.title: Reconnect & Sync
items.body: WebSocket drops → exponential backoff. On reconnection, sync missed messages using the last timestamp/cursor.

items.title: Typing & presence
items.body: Debounce typing events; send "typing" status at most every few seconds. Presence via periodic heartbeat.

blocks.title: Ensure no data loss.
blocks.body: Each message has a client-generated ID (idempotency key). When resending due to network loss, the same ID is used, and the server handles deduplication. Combine a local queue with IndexedDB so that messages composed offline are sent when connectivity is restored.

blocks.title: O — Evaluation Criteria
blocks.body: The interviewer wants to hear your considerations: WebSocket vs SSE (chat requires two-way communication → WebSocket), how to handle incoming messages while scrolling through history, and resolving message order conflicts (sort by server timestamp).

blocks.question: Why should you use a client-generated id (idempotency key) for each message?
options: So that when resending (due to network loss), the server can recognize it and avoid creating duplicate messages.
To avoid needing a database on the server.
blocks.explanation: When the network is unstable, a client may resend a message that has already reached the server but has not yet received an acknowledgment. Using the same idempotency key helps the server deduplicate requests, ensuring exactly-once delivery.

sections.title: Case Study: Autocomplete / Typeahead
sections.intro: A small but extremely common problem because it tests: debounce, race condition, caching, and keyboard accessibility — all in one component.

blocks.caption: Flow from key press to result

blocks.label: JS

blocks.title: Accessibility — don't forget.
blocks.body: Use the combobox pattern: `aria-expanded`, `aria-activedescendant` pointing to the currently selected item, navigation with ↑↓, Enter to select, Esc to close. This is a point many candidates overlook, but interviewers pay close attention to.

blocks.title: Query caching
blocks.body: Cache results by query (Map). When the user deletes and retypes the same word, return immediately from cache — no API call. Combine with debounce to significantly reduce the number of requests.

blocks.question: In autocomplete, a race condition occurs when the response from an earlier request arrives after the response from a later request, causing the UI to display outdated results. The correct way to handle this is to use a cancellation mechanism, such as an AbortController in fetch, or by tracking the latest request with a counter or timestamp to ignore stale responses.
options: When typing too slowly; handle by increasing debounce.
When the response of an old query arrives after a new query and overwrites it; handle by using AbortController to cancel the old request.
When the cache is too large; clear the cache.
When the dropdown is too long; virtualize the list.
blocks.explanation: A request sent earlier may return later than a later request, causing old results to overwrite new ones. Canceling the old request with AbortController (or ignoring responses that don't match the current query) is the standard approach.

sections.title: Case Study: Photo Gallery (like Google Photos)
sections.intro: The problem of smoothly displaying thousands of images. Check: virtualization for the grid, lazy loading, responsive images, and lightbox. The core principle is "only work with what the user sees."

items.title: Responsive thumbnail
items.body: The server generates multiple sizes; srcset ensures each device loads the correct resolution. The lightbox only loads the full version.

items.title: Lazy + placeholder
items.body: Display average color or blur-up (LQIP) while loading → creates a fast feel and prevents layout shifts.

items.title: Reuse DOM node
items.body: When scrolling, reuse virtual nodes that have left the screen instead of creating new ones → reduces GC pressure.

items.title: Lightbox preload
items.body: When opening an image, preload the previous/next image to enable smooth transitions without waiting.

blocks.title: O — Points the interviewer pays attention to
blocks.body: How to calculate grid layout when images have different aspect ratios (justified layout like Google Photos requires knowing dimensions in advance — server returns width/height), and how to maintain scroll position when loading more images above.

blocks.question: Why is virtualization important for a gallery of 10,000 images?
options: Only render images within the viewport → lightweight DOM, smooth scrolling, memory does not bloat with the number of images.
blocks.explanation: Rendering all 10,000 image nodes makes the DOM heavy, consumes memory, and causes lag when scrolling. Virtualization renders only the visible portion, keeping the DOM lightweight regardless of the total number of images.

sections.title: Case Study: Infinite Data Table
sections.intro: A massive data table (like Airtable, financial spreadsheets) with millions of rows and many columns. Check: bidirectional virtualization, server-side sort/filter, sticky header/column, and inline editing.

blocks.code:
# Do not load all 1 million rows. Use server-side sort/filter/paginate; the client only requests a "window" of data.
GET /rows?sort=price:desc&filter=active&offset=4000&limit=100

# Or cursor-based for continuously changing data.
GET /rows?after=cursor_abc&limit=100
blocks.label: HTTP

blocks.title: Why use sort/filter on the server side?
blocks.body: Sorting 1 million rows on the client will block the main thread. The server (with database indexing) handles this quickly and only returns the page of data the client needs. The client should only sort/filter locally when the dataset is small and fully loaded.

blocks.title: **Trap: dynamic height**
blocks.body: If each row has a different height (text wrap), virtualization becomes more difficult — you need to measure and cache actual heights, or use estimation with adjustment techniques. Fixed row height is much simpler.

blocks.question: With a table of 1 million rows, where should sorting/filtering be done?
options: Load everything to the client and sort using JavaScript.
On the server (with an indexed database), the client only requests a page of sorted/filtered data.
blocks.explanation: Loading and sorting 1 million rows on the client freezes the UI. A server with an index processes quickly and only returns the "window" of data the client is viewing. Client-side sorting is only suitable for small datasets.

sections.title: Case Study: Video Player (like YouTube)
sections.intro: Smooth video playback at all network speeds. Tests understanding of adaptive bitrate streaming (HLS/DASH), buffering, and building custom controls with accessibility.

blocks.caption: The video is divided into chunks of multiple qualities, and the player selects based on network conditions.

rows: Two streaming standards divide video into chunks + manifest. HLS is popular on Apple, DASH is an open standard.
Preload several chunks to prevent lag when the network fluctuates.
Start at a low level to play fast, then gradually increase as the network is measured.
The browser API that allows JavaScript to "load" chunks into a video element — the foundation of an ABR player.

blocks.title: The core trade-off of ABR
blocks.body: High quality vs. rebuffering risk. ABR algorithm balances: prioritizes no interruptions (rebuffering severely harms user experience) over always delivering maximum quality. Starting low for fast playback is the standard strategy.

blocks.title: A11y for player
blocks.body: Controls must be keyboard accessible (Space for play/pause, ←→ for seek), include ARIA labels, support captions, and must not autoplay with sound (to avoid annoyance and a11y violations).

blocks.question: When the user's network drops mid-stream, Adaptive Bitrate Streaming (ABR) works by detecting the decrease in available bandwidth through client-side monitoring. The player then automatically switches to a lower-bitrate video segment, reducing quality to prevent buffering. This process is seamless, as the video is divided into small chunks encoded at multiple bitrates, and the player requests the next chunk at the appropriate level based on real-time network conditions.
options: Stop the video and report the error.
The next chunk is loaded at a lower quality level, and the video continues without interruption.
Reload the entire video from the beginning.
blocks.explanation: The video is divided into chunks at multiple quality levels. When the network drops, the player selects the next chunk at a lower quality to maintain continuous playback — prioritizing no buffering over maximum quality.

sections.intro: The hardest and most "valuable" problem: multiple users editing the same document in real-time without overwriting each other. Tests understanding of OT vs CRDT, presence, and conflict resolution.

blocks.caption: Two people editing simultaneously — how to avoid overwriting?

rows: Transform operations concurrently to maintain consistency.
Self-merging data structure, no transformation needed.
Do we need a central server?
Complex logic transformations are difficult to get right.
Simpler in theory, but requires more metadata.

items.title: Presence
items.body: Other users' cursor & selection sent via private channel (no persistence needed), throttle to reduce traffic.

items.title: Offline editing
items.body: Save local operations when offline; when back online, merge using CRDT/OT — no data loss.

items.title: Version history
items.body: Save snapshot + operation sequence to rewind history and restore.

items.title: Local-first
items.body: Apply changes locally immediately (optimistic), sync in the background → no typing lag.

blocks.title: Why not use "last write wins"?
blocks.body: The simplest approach — last writer wins — will lose the other person's text. OT/CRDT ensures both concurrent operations are preserved and all clients converge to the same state. This is the difference between a "toy" and a real product.

blocks.title: O — How to make an impression
blocks.body: State that this is a "convergence in distributed systems" problem, compare OT vs CRDT with clear trade-offs, and mention local-first + offline. No need for transform algorithm code — understanding the principles and trade-offs is sufficient for the FE round.

blocks.question: Why does collaborative editing NOT use the "last write wins" strategy?
options: Because it overwrites other people's changes; OT/CRDT preserves all concurrent operations and ensures convergence.
Because the browser does not support it.
Because it consumes a lot of memory.
blocks.explanation: "Last write wins" causes one person's operation to overwrite another's → data loss. OT and CRDT merge concurrent operations so that no data is lost and all clients converge to the same result.

sections.title: Case Study: Designing a News Feed
sections.intro: Applying RADIO to a classic problem: designing a Facebook/Twitter-style feed. This is a template you can reuse for most feed-based questions.

items.title: Functional
items.body: View feed (infinite scroll), create posts, like/comment, display images/videos, update when new posts appear.

items.title: Non-functional
items.body: Fast loading on mobile + 3G, smooth scrolling, supports a11y, and can handle intermittent network connectivity.

blocks.label: Normalized state
API contract

items: Smooth scrolling: virtualization keeps the DOM lightweight even with an infinite feed.
Load more: IntersectionObserver on the last sentinel in the list → fetch the next page.
Image: lazy-load, use srcset/responsive, and blur placeholder to avoid CLS.
Instant interaction: optimistic UI for likes/comments, rollback on error.
New post: SSE signals "new post" → show pill, do not auto-insert to avoid layout shift.
Offline/error: display old cache + skeleton, retry with backoff.
Code splitting: separate PostComposer and media viewer from the main bundle.
A11y: aria-live for new posts, keyboard navigation for actions.

blocks.title: How to "score points" in the O part
blocks.body: Don’t list everything. Pick 2–3 optimizations most relevant to the finalized requirements (e.g., because smoothness on mobile is needed → virtualization + responsive images) and dive deep into them. Depth over breadth.

blocks.question: In the News Feed, why should we show a "X new posts" pill instead of automatically inserting new posts at the top of the list?
options: Avoid layout shift (CLS) to prevent users from losing their reading position; give them control.
Since SSE does not allow automatic insertion
blocks.explanation: Auto-inserting content at the top pushes the user's current reading position down (bad CLS, causes annoyance). Use a pill so they can actively tap when ready — much better UX.

blocks.caption: Suggested time allocation

items.title: Performance vs Complexity
items.body: Which optimizations are worth the effort? Measure before optimizing.

items.title: CSR vs SSR
items.body: Balance between SEO/front-end performance and server cost/complexity.

items.title: Real-time vs Simple
items.body: WebSocket is powerful but complex; polling is sufficient for many cases.

items.title: Cache vs Freshness
items.body: Stale-while-revalidate balances both.

blocks.title: Signs of a strong candidate
blocks.body: (1) Ask clarifying questions before solving. (2) Draw clear diagrams. (3) Always mention trade-offs, never say "always use X". (4) Proactively address a11y, error handling, and performance. (5) Manage time wisely, know when to dive deep.

items.front: RADIO stands for "Radio Detection and Ranging".
items.back: Requirements · Architecture · Data model · Interface (API) · Optimizations.
Server state · Local state · Global UI state · URL state. Each type has its own tools.

items.front: What are the 3 Core Web Vitals?

Optimistic UI is a design pattern where the user interface immediately reflects the expected outcome of an action, without waiting for confirmation from the server. This creates a faster, more responsive user experience. If the server later rejects the action, the UI rolls back to the previous state and displays an error.
items.back: Update the UI immediately before the server responds, rollback on error → instant feel.
For two-way, low-latency communication: chat, collaboration, gaming, trading.
Escape output, avoid dangerouslySetInnerHTML, sanitize (DOMPurify), CSP, HttpOnly cookies.

sections.title: Question bank summary
sections.intro: Mix multiple topics to test overall knowledge. Answer first, then view the explanation. Try to explain "why" yourself before clicking.

blocks.question: The biggest issue with pure CSR for a marketing website is what?
options: Poor SEO and slow initial content display (white screen while loading JS).
blocks.explanation: CSR returns an empty HTML shell, then loads JS and fetches data, causing an initial white screen and difficulty for crawlers → bad for the SEO of marketing pages.

blocks.question: Which technique is most effective at reducing initial JS load time for large apps?
options: Code splitting + lazy loading by route/component
blocks.explanation: Code splitting only loads the JavaScript needed for the current screen, significantly reducing the initial bundle size → faster FCP/LCP.

blocks.question: GraphQL best solves the problem of over-fetching and under-fetching data in REST APIs.
options: Over-fetching / under-fetching — the client declares exactly the fields it needs.
blocks.explanation: GraphQL allows clients to fetch exactly the data they need with a single query, reducing over/under-fetching. However, caching is more complex compared to REST.

blocks.question: Where should the selected filter and tab be stored so that sharing the link and the back/forward buttons work correctly?
blocks.explanation: State that needs to be "shareable" and synchronized with browser history should be placed in the URL. When users copy the link, they get the exact state.

blocks.question: The lightest and most suitable mechanism for displaying push notifications from the server is?
options: SSE — server pushes one-way, auto-reconnect, runs over HTTP.
Cannot be done on the web.
blocks.explanation: Notification only requires server-to-client (one-way). SSE is lighter than WebSocket, has auto-reconnect, and runs over standard HTTP.

blocks.question: Which attribute is used to announce dynamic content (e.g., "added to cart") to screen readers?
options: I'm sorry, but you didn't provide any Vietnamese text for me to translate. Please provide the Vietnamese technical interview prep text you'd like me to translate into natural English.
blocks.explanation: aria-live tells the screen reader to read content when that region changes without requiring focus. Polite waits until the current reading finishes, while assertive interrupts immediately.

blocks.question: The most secure way to store session tokens against XSS is?
blocks.explanation: localStorage/JS variables can both be read by scripts → dangerous if XSS occurs. HttpOnly cookies prevent JS from reading them, and combining Secure + SameSite mitigates both XSS and CSRF.

blocks.question: Reading `element.offsetHeight` immediately after changing the styles of multiple elements will cause what?
options: Forced synchronous layout — layout thrashing if repeated.
blocks.explanation: Reading layout properties forces the browser to recalculate layout immediately to return accurate values. Interleaving reads and writes in a loop causes thrashing. Batch reads first, then writes.

blocks.question: Between Promise.then and setTimeout(fn, 0), which runs first?
options: Promise.then — because microtasks are cleared before macrotasks.
blocks.explanation: After each macrotask, the event loop clears the entire microtask queue before fetching the next macrotask. Promises (microtasks) always run before setTimeout (macrotasks).

blocks.question: According to the testing pyramid, unit tests should make up the largest number of tests.
blocks.explanation: The testing pyramid recommends many unit/integration tests (fast, cheap) at the bottom, and few E2E tests (slow, expensive, prone to flakiness) at the top.

blocks.question: The core difference between "lab data" and "field data" is what?
options: Lab = stable simulated environment (Lighthouse); Field = measured from real users (RUM/CrUX)
Lab is always more precise than field.
The field only runs on localhost.
blocks.explanation: Lab data is good for debugging under controlled conditions; field data reflects real user experiences across diverse devices/networks and is what Google uses for ranking.

blocks.question: Tree-shaking requires ES module syntax (import/export) to work effectively, as it relies on static analysis to determine which exports are used and which can be removed.
options: Code must be written in TypeScript.
Use ES modules (static import/export) so the bundler can analyze unused code.
blocks.explanation: Tree-shaking relies on static analysis of ES module import/export to eliminate dead code. Dynamic CommonJS is difficult to analyze, resulting in poor tree-shaking.

blocks.question: The most common cause of CLS (Cumulative Layout Shift) related to images is missing explicit width and height attributes on image elements.
options: Images without declared width/height cause content to jump when the image finishes loading.
blocks.explanation: Without dimensions, the browser does not reserve space, and content below gets pushed down when the image loads → CLS. Declare width/height or aspect-ratio.

blocks.question: What is the role of a Service Worker?
options: A background proxy intercepts requests, manages cache, and enables offline support and push notifications.
blocks.explanation: A Service Worker is a background script that sits between the app and the network, controlling cache and requests — forming the foundation for offline-first functionality and push notifications.

blocks.question: Why should chat use WebSocket instead of SSE?
options: Chat requires real-time two-way communication; WebSocket supports both sending and receiving, while SSE is only one-way server-to-client.
SSE does not run on the browser.
WebSocket does not require a connection.
blocks.explanation: Continuous chat sends and receives from both sides. WebSocket is a full-duplex channel that fits this use case; SSE only pushes one-way, so it's more suitable for notifications/feeds.

blocks.title: In conclusion
blocks.body: FE System Design has no single correct answer. Practice the mindset of: clarify → structure → trade-offs → deep dive. Revisit the diagrams and flashcards here regularly; spaced repetition is the best way to retain knowledge. Wishing you a successful interview! 🚀
